summary: Patrick McKenzie announces plans to start a new software project over the summer, a subscription-based web app targeting a specific vertical market. He estimates a $1,500 launch budget with modest monthly advertising spend, and projects the app could exceed his salary within a year. He plans to build it on LAMP, hire a lawyer and professional designer, and be as transparent about the process as he has been with Bingo Card Creator.
key_points: The new project is a subscription-based web app with a 1-2 month free trial, targeting a specific vertical with the value proposition of making customers more money
He projects exceeding his current salary within a year of launch with modest customer volume assumptions and a $1,500 launch budget
He acknowledges key risks: unfamiliarity with web development, less domain expertise than his teaching vertical, and dependency on a major corporation
He believes he has three distinct competitive advantages over cheaper alternatives that are readily apparent to customers
money_quotes: I'm going to make it easier for you to make a lot of money. You're going to pay me a trivial amount every month and be happy to do it. We'll be doing business together for YEARS.
The better option of the two, from my current research, would (with rather modest assumptions made about customer volume) probably exceed my current salary within a year of launch.

I have competition offering services which can accomplish the same general goal for cheaper, but I have at least three ideas why my method is uniquely superior, in a way which is readily apparent to the customer.
I will be unhealthily dependent on a major corporation whose name, for once, does NOT start with G.
This is sort of a memo to myself to not spend summer on videogames.
key_insight: A subscription web app that demonstrably helps customers make money can command loyal, long-term paying customers even against cheaper competition, provided the value proposition is clearly superior.
